question: In copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC), what is the role of the copper(I) catalyst?
options: It activates the azide group for nucleophilic attack
It coordinates both azide and alkyne, lowering the activation energy for the [3+2] cycloaddition
It serves as a leaving group during product formation
explanation: Cu(I) coordinates the terminal alkyne, generating a copper acetylide intermediate that undergoes stepwise cycloaddition with the azide. This lowers the activation energy compared to the uncatalyzed thermal Huisgen reaction, enabling regioselective 1,4-disubstituted triazole formation.
